content.description:
**Status:** Proposal

## Summary

Pages should be Hob expressions, not markdown strings. This eliminates the need for a new AST format, a new parser, or a transclusion-specific extension system. Hob already is a structured, code-as-data representation — page structure is just another thing it can express.

## The Problem

Today, notes store their body as `content.description` — a markdown string. This works for simple prose but has fundamental limitations:

- **Transclusions are markdown-specific.** The `![[item-id]]` syntax is handled by a markdown-it plugin that does string interpolation at render time. It's not reactive — if the transcluded item changes, the embedding doesn't update.
- **Markdown is opaque text.** The system can't reason about page structure. You can't query "which pages embed this item" without parsing markdown. You can't programmatically compose pages. You can't have inline computed values.
- **No reactive inline values.** There's no way to embed a live value (item count, field reference, computed result) that updates when the underlying data changes.
- **Extension requires parser hacking.** Every new page-level feature (transclusion, computed values, interactive widgets) requires a new markdown-it plugin, new regex patterns, and new edge cases.

The instinct is to design a richer AST — a document tree with nodes for paragraphs, transclusions, computed values, etc. But Hobson already has such a tree: Hob expressions.

## The Insight

A page is a Hob expression that evaluates to DOM. No new format, no new parser — just new library functions.

Hob already provides:
- **Structured data** (JSON AST — lists, vectors, maps, strings, symbols)
- **Evaluation** (the Hob evaluator)
- **DOM generation** (`hiccup->dom`)
- **Reactivity** (`get-item` with selectors, `render-item`)
- **Code-as-data** (queryable, transformable, inspectable)

A "structured page" is simply a Hob expression that uses page-oriented library functions instead of (or alongside) view-oriented ones.

## The Primitives

Four library functions define the page vocabulary. All are ordinary Hob functions — not evaluator primitives, not special forms.

### `text`

A block of prose. Takes a markdown string and returns DOM. This is where markdown-it continues to be useful — for inline formatting within a prose block, not as the page-level structure.

```clojure
(text "This is a paragraph with **bold** and *italic* text.")

(text "## Section Heading

Multiple paragraphs work naturally.
Markdown handles inline formatting.")
```

### `transclude`

Embed another item, reactively. When the source item changes, the transclusion re-renders.

```clojure
(transclude @my-note)

;; With options
(transclude @my-note {:view @custom-view :depth 1})
```

Under the hood, `transclude` calls `render-item` — it's the same rendering infrastructure that spatial-canvas and generic-view already use. The reactive dependency tracking comes for free.

### `val`

An inline reactive value. Evaluates a Hob expression and displays the result. Re-evaluates when dependencies change.

```clojure
(text (str "You have " (val (count (query-tagged @project-tag))) " items in this project."))

(text (str "Last modified: " (val (get-field @my-note :modified))))
```

`val` is the key primitive that markdown can never provide — an inline live computation that participates in Hobson's reactivity system.

### `link`

A navigable reference to an item. Unlike markdown's `item://` URLs (which are rendered by a markdown-it plugin), `link` is a first-class Hob expression that the system can query and reason about.

```clojure
(link @my-note)                    ;; displays item name, navigates on click
(link @my-note "custom label")     ;; custom display text
```

## Page Examples

A page is a `do` block (or a `page` macro that wraps `do` with page-level styling):

```clojure
(do
  (text "# Project Status

  Overview of the current project state.")

  (text (str "Total items: " (val (count (query-tagged @project-tag)))))

  (text "## Key Documents")
  (transclude @architecture-overview)
  (transclude @design-decisions)

  (text "## Related")
  (link @roadmap)
  (link @open-questions))
```

This is a complete page. It has prose, live computed values, reactive transclusions, and navigable links. It's also a valid Hob expression — queryable, transformable, storable as JSON AST.

## Why Library Functions

These primitives are **library functions**, not evaluator builtins. This matters:

- **Minimal kernel philosophy.** The evaluator doesn't need to know about pages. Pages are a userland concept built from existing primitives (`hiccup->dom`, `render-item`, `get-item`).
- **Iteration speed.** Library functions can be modified, extended, and replaced without touching the kernel. A user can write their own `text` variant with different styling.
- **Composability.** Because they're ordinary functions, they compose with everything else in Hob — `map`, `filter`, `let`, conditionals. A page can contain logic:

```clojure
(if (> (count items) 0)
  (map transclude items)
  (text "*No items yet.*"))
```

- **No new parser.** The Hob reader already knows how to parse these expressions. The evaluator already knows how to evaluate them. The JSON AST already knows how to store them.

## Editor Strategy

### Immediate: Structural Editor

A structural editor for Hob expressions works immediately for structured pages. You're editing a tree of function calls — add a `text` block, insert a `transclude`, wrap something in an `if`. This is the same structural editing that the [JSON AST proposal](item://c0c0c0c0-0051-0000-0000-000000000000) enables for code.

### Future: Prose-Feeling Editor

The harder problem is making page editing feel like writing prose rather than programming. This is a separate concern from the data representation. Possible approaches:

- **Block-level WYSIWYG** where each `text` block is a contenteditable region, and the page structure (transclusions, vals) is shown as inline chips or blocks between prose regions.
- **Hybrid editor** that presents the page as flowing text with embedded computed regions, compiling edits back to Hob expressions.

This is explicitly deferred. The structural editor is sufficient to validate the approach. A prose editor is a UX layer on top, not a prerequisite.

## Migration Path

The transition from markdown to structured pages is gradual:

1. **Current:** `content.description` contains a markdown string. Notes render via markdown-it.
2. **Transition:** Notes can have either `content.description` (markdown, legacy) or `content.hob` (Hob expression, structured page). Views detect which is present.
3. **Conversion:** A `text` block wrapping the entire markdown string is a valid structured page. Migration can be automated: `(text <entire-markdown-string>)`. This preserves all existing content exactly.
4. **Enrichment:** Over time, pages are refactored to use `transclude`, `val`, and `link` where appropriate, gaining reactivity and structure.

Markdown-it continues to be used — it renders the *contents* of `text` blocks. What changes is that markdown is no longer the page-level structure. It handles inline formatting (bold, italic, code, lists) within a prose block, while Hob handles the page-level composition.

## What This Enables

- **Reactive computed fields** across items — spreadsheet-like calculations where formulas reference item properties instead of cell addresses. See [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) for the full proposal.
- **Reactive transclusions** that update when the source changes — using the same dependency tracking that views already use.
- **Live computed values** inline in prose — item counts, field references, dates, aggregations.
- **Queryable page structure** — "find all pages that transclude this item" is a walk over JSON AST, not regex over markdown.
- **Programmatic page generation** — dashboards, reports, indices generated by Hob expressions.
- **Composable pages** — pages that include other pages, conditional sections, data-driven content.
- **Structural editing** for pages — same infrastructure as code editing.

## What This Does Not Do

- **Replace markdown for inline formatting.** Bold, italic, code, lists, headings — these stay in markdown, rendered by markdown-it within `text` blocks.
- **Require learning Hob to write notes.** A note that is a single `(text "...")` block is functionally identical to today's markdown note. The complexity is opt-in.
- **Change the item model.** Pages are still items. `content.hob` already exists for Hob code items. This just uses it for a new purpose.

## Alternative: Enhance Markdown Incrementally

The structured pages proposal replaces a working composition system — markdown with [rich transclusion support](item://c0c0c0c0-0035-0000-0000-000000000000) (full/partial/functional/query) — with Hob expressions. The current system is deeply used: transclusions are inline within prose, not separate blocks, and the editing experience (CodeMirror + Insert Link/Transclusion toolbar) works well.

Replacing this has two costs:

1. **Editor regression.** The structural editor is natural for code but painful for prose. A block editor (CodeMirror instances separated by transclusion cards) would force inline transclusions into block-level structure, breaking the flow of existing documents.
2. **Rebuilding richness.** The current transclusion system supports field extraction, region markers, symbol resolution, functional transclusion (`?call=`), query blocks, and multiple chrome modes. All of this would need to be re-implemented as Hob library functions.

An alternative approach adds the missing capabilities *to* the existing markdown system:

### 1. Reactive Transclusions

`hobson-markdown` already renders transclusions as DOM nodes. Make those mount points subscribe to the transcluded item's changes and re-render when the source updates. The reactive infrastructure exists — views already do this. The data model and editor stay untouched.

### 2. Inline Computed Values

Add a `{{hob-expression}}` syntax via a new markdown-it plugin. Evaluates a Hob expression at render time, subscribes to dependencies, updates reactively. Authored as inline text in CodeMirror — no editor changes needed.

### 3. Queryable References

Build an index of `item://` references by parsing them out of `content.description` at save time. Less elegant than walking a JSON AST, but pragmatic and doesn't require changing how content is authored.

### Trade-offs

| | Structured Pages | Enhanced Markdown |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Clean — one representation for code and pages | Pragmatic — two systems (Hob for code, markdown for prose) |
| Editor | Requires new editor (structural editor is a prose regression) | Keeps working CodeMirror + transclusion toolbar |
| Migration | All existing content needs conversion | Zero migration — existing content gains new capabilities |
| Composability | Full Hob expressiveness (conditionals, loops, programmatic pages) | Limited to what markdown-it plugins can express |
| Queryability | Native — walk JSON AST | Index-based — parse `item://` URLs at save time |
| Inline computation | Native — `val` is a Hob expression | Plugin-based — `{{expression}}` syntax |
| Extension model | Write Hob functions | Write markdown-it plugins (more constrained) |

The enhanced markdown approach preserves a rich, working system while gaining the key benefits (reactivity, computed values, queryable references). Structured pages remain the architecturally superior long-term direction, but require solving the editor problem first — and that editor needs to match or exceed the current prose authoring experience before it can replace it.

## References

- [Hob Language Design Direction](item://c0c0c0c0-0058-0000-0000-000000000000) — The north star for Hob as the primary user-programming language in Hobson.
- [JSON AST as Canonical Code Representation](item://c0c0c0c0-0051-0000-0000-000000000000) — The foundation that makes structured pages possible: code (and now pages) as queryable, structured JSON.
- [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) — The data-layer complement to `val`: computed fields that evaluate Hob formulas on access, enabling spreadsheet-like reactive calculations across items.
- [Rich Text Editing](item://c0c0c0c0-0041-0000-0000-000000000000) — The earlier idea item exploring rich text. Structured pages subsume this by making the page structure programmable rather than relying on a rich text editor framework.
